summary: This lesson introduces the present perfect tense using haber (to have, in the auxiliary sense). The forms of haber are taught using the mnemonic of its two vowels (e→he/hemos, a→ha/has/han). The past participle for -ar verbs is formed with -ado. This tense is used more widely in Spanish than English.

teachings.conceptName: Haber: auxiliary 'to have' for past tense
teachings.summary: Haber means 'to have' in the sense of 'I have eaten' (not possession, which is tener). It is extremely irregular. The forms can be remembered by the two vowels in haber: /e/ gives he (I have) and hemos (we have); /a/ gives ha (he/she/it/you formal has), has (you informal have), han (they have).
teachings.rule: haber vowels: e → he (I have), hemos (we have). a → ha (he/she/you formal have), has (you informal have), han (they have). I and we are connected (he→hemos). The rest build from ha: ha, has, han.
teachings.notes: Haber is spelled h-e: the silent h comes from haber. Only two verbs form 'I' this way: sé from saber, and he from haber. This 'have' past is used even more in Spanish than English — you can use it where English would use simple past.

teachings.summary: To form the past participle needed after haber, -ar verbs drop -ar and add -ado. This is much simpler than English where past participles are highly irregular (eaten, gone, wanted).
teachings.rule: For -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -ado to get the past participle (hablar → hablado, tomar → tomado).

examples.spanish: Lo hemos anticipado por mucho tiempo
examples.english: We have anticipated it for a long time

teachings.summary: With haber + past participle, there are two changed verbs together (he hablado), so object pronouns can only go before the first changed verb. You cannot attach them to the participle.
teachings.rule: With haber constructions, object pronouns go before haber: lo he terminado (not he lo terminado or he terminadolo).

teachings.summary: Por means 'for' when talking about periods of time. Also appears in 'por favor' (please, literally 'for a favour').

examples.spanish: Lo hemos anticipado por mucho tiempo
examples.english: We have anticipated it for a long time

teachings.summary: Tiempo means time. Related to the English musical term 'tempo'. The e in tempo splits to ie under stress, giving tiempo.
